# Sampling with minimizers {#sec-intro-minimizers}

The sketching methods presented in @sec-intro-sketching compress a sequence into a compact summary, but they treat the sequence as a simple "bag" of /kmers: two sequences with a high Jaccard index are considered similar regardless of *where* the shared /kmers appear.

Many bioinformatics tasks require something stronger: a guarantee that two sequences sharing a long local exact match will also share at least one selected /kmer at corresponding positions.
This *locality* property is what distinguishes minimizers from general "context-free" sketching: here selected /kmers act as representatives of their local context.

## Definition and fundamental properties {#sec-minimizer-definition}

The concept of minimizers was introduced independently by two groups in 2003--2004.
@schleimer2003winnowing introduced *winnowing* in the context of document fingerprinting and plagiarism detection: a sliding window scans the document and the smallest /kmer of each window (according to some ordering) is selected as a fingerprint.
@roberts2004reducing proposed the same idea for biological sequences, introducing the term *minimizer*, with the explicit goal of providing a locality-preserving sketch: any two sequences sharing a long exact match are guaranteed to share at least one minimizer.

A minimizer scheme is parameterized by three values: the /kmer length $k$, the window size $w$, and a total ordering $\prec$ on /kmers.
A *window* of size $w$ is a set of $w$ consecutive /kmers, spanning a substring of length $\ell = w + k - 1$.
The *minimizer* of a window is the /kmer that is smallest according to $\prec$, with ties broken by selecting the leftmost occurrence^[Choosing the leftmost occurrence is a convention, some schemes use the rightmost one or select all tied /kmers.].
Sliding the window one position at a time along the sequence yields the *minimizer sketch* of that sequence: the (deduplicated) sequence of minimizer positions.

:::{.callout-caution}
## Different notations

Depending on the context, multiple notations are used in the literature.
The parameters that we defined above as $k$ and $\ell$ are sometimes denoted $m$ and $k$ respectively, meaning that one minimizer of size $m$ is selected from each substring of size $k = w + m - 1$.
:::

:::{#fig-minimizers-overview}
![](../fig/intro/minimizers)

Example of lexicographic minimizer selection, for $k=3$ and $w=5$.
Each line corresponds to a sliding window of 5 consecutive 3-mers, selecting a minimizer highlighted in red.
The starting positions of the 3 minimizers are marked by red arrows above the sequence.
Consecutive windows selecting the same minimizer position can be merged into a single /skmer.
:::

An essential property of minimizers is the so-called *window guarantee*.

::: {#pty-window-guarantee}
## Window guarantee

Any window of *w* consecutive /kmers, i.e. any substring of length $\ell = w + k - 1$, contains at least one minimizer.
:::

This property has two important consequences that make minimizers especially useful in practice.

::: {#pty-conservation}
## Conservation

Any pair of sequences sharing an exact match of length at least $\ell = w + k - 1$ will share at least one minimizer.
:::

::: {#pty-bounded-dist}
## Bounded distance

The distance between consecutive minimizers is at most $w$.
:::

These two properties together ensure that minimizers can serve as *anchors* for sequence comparison: shared minimizers between a query and a reference indicate candidate matching positions, and the bounded distance guarantees that no long match is missed [@minimizers-survey-ndiaye].

## Density and orderings {#sec-minimizer-orderings}

The *density* of a minimizer scheme is the expected fraction of /kmers selected over all positions of a random sequence.
Since consecutive minimizers are at most $w$ positions apart (@pty-bounded-dist), the density is always lower-bounded by $1/w$.
We'll see in the following subsections that the ordering used to compare /kmers can have a big impact on the density, and thus on the size of the resulting sketch.

### Lexicographic ordering

The simplest ordering we can use is the lexicographic order on /kmer strings.
While it requires no precomputation and is straightforward to implement, lexicographic ordering has well-known limitations: homopolymers (especially repeated `A`s) tend to select adjacent /kmers, thus increasing the density and skewing the distribution of minimizer values [@minimizers-ordering;@minimizers-survey-zheng].
In particular, @minimizers-ordering report an empirical density of $2.24 / (w+1)$ for lexicographic minimizers with $k=10$ and $w=10$.
An example of lexicographic minimizer selection is given in @fig-minimizers-overview.

### Random ordering

Another popular solution is to order /kmers randomly, typically using a pseudo-random hash function.
@miniception proved that random minimizers achieve a density of $2 / (w+1) + o(1/w)$ as long as $k > 3 \log_{|\Sigma|}(w+1)$.
In practice this condition is almost always satisfied and random ordering is the default choice in most bioinformatics tools.

This gap between the density of $2 / (w+1)$ and the $1/w$ lower bound motivated the design of improved orderings and low-density schemes, which we discuss in more details in @sec-low-density-minimizers.

## /Skmers and sequence partitioning {#sec-skmers}

A key practical use of minimizers is to *partition* a sequence into overlapping segments called */skmers*.
For each minimizer $x$ of the sequence, the corresponding /skmer is the substring covering the consecutive windows that select $x$ as a minimizer.
An example of /skmer is depicted in @fig-minimizers-overview.
Note that, in the context of /skmers, the minimizer size is often denoted $m$ while $k$ denotes the number of bases in a window, i.e. $w + m - 1$.
Using these notations, two consecutive /skmers always overlap by $k - 1$ bases.

## Improving conservation {#sec-minimizer-conservation}

### Syncmers

### Strobemers

## Practical applications {#sec-minimizer-applications}

### Read alignment

### Sequence classification and metagenomics

### Partitioning indexes

### Genome assembly

---

TBA

- historical context [@schleimer2003winnowing;@roberts2004reducing]
- random vs lexicographic
- practical use cases [@minimizers-survey-ndiaye]
- improving conservation [@syncmers;@shaw-conservation;@strobemers;@strobealign]
- low density schemes detailed in @sec-low-density-minimizers
